trait.name: Source
trait.text: Stranger Things: Welcome to the Hellfire Club

note.name: Read
note.text:
A black-barked tree looms crookedly over this eerie crypt. The tree's gnarled branches press against the fifteen-foot-high ceiling, and its twisted trunk rises from the splintered lid of a closed coffin encaged in knotted roots. Squirming vines wriggle along the crypt's walls.
Three hideous dogs with eyeless faces growl as they pace around the tree.

note.name: Info
note.text: This crypt once belonged to Gunthar the Gluttonous. The tree that protrudes from Gunthar's coffin is a Gulthias tree, sprouted from the stake plunged into the vampire's chest. Though the vampire is long vanquished, the tree's magic resurrected his three loyal hounds and transformed them into Demodogs. The Demodogs are Hostile toward all non-Plant creatures and defend their master's remains.

note.name: Gulthias Tree
note.text:
This Gulthias tree is a Huge object that has AC 15; HP 50; Immunity to Necrotic, Poison, and Psychic damage; and Vulnerability to Fire damage. If the tree is reduced to 0 Hit Points, read or paraphrase the following text:
"The tree screeches as it withers and dies. Silence falls over the crypt, but the tree remains defiantly in place, its roots anchored deep into the earth."
Unless the Gulthias tree is uprooted, it regrows in 3d10 days. As a Study action, a character can examine the tree and make a DC 13 Intelligence (Arcana) check. On a successful check, the character knows the Gulthias tree will regrow if left in the earth.
To uproot the tree, a character must succeed on a DC 15 Strength (Athletics) check. If Rilly June is with the characters, she takes the Help action to assist them with this endeavor.
If the tree is uprooted, its effects end immediately, and all remaining Needle Blights and Vine Blight Tanglers within the tunnels die.

note.name: Treasure
note.text:
After uprooting the Gulthias tree, the characters can access the contents of Gunthar's wrecked coffin. Inside are the vampire's desiccated corpse and the following items:
A +1 Dart in the shape of a strange pollywog
An amulet sporting a delicate crystal spider, which is worth 25 GP
A blue silk handkerchief with gold embroidery, which is worth 5 GP
A pouch that contains 50 GP
Fearing these items were cursed, the adventurers who slew Gunthar refused to take them.

item.name: Dart +1 [5.5e]
item.text:
You have a +1 bonus to attack and damage rolls made with this magic weapon.
Vex (Mastery). If you hit a creature with this weapon and deal damage to the creature, you have Advantage on your next attack roll against that creature before the end of your next turn.
Finesse. When making an attack with a Finesse weapon, use your choice of your Strength or Dexterity modifier for the attack and damage rolls. You must use the same modifier for both rolls.
Thrown. If a weapon has the Thrown property, you can throw the weapon to make a ranged attack, and you can draw that weapon as part of the attack. If the weapon is a Melee weapon, use the same ability modifier for the attack and damage rolls that you use for a melee attack with that weapon.
Source: Dungeon Master's Guide 2024 p. 324

item.name: Crystal Spider Amulet
item.text: Source: Stranger Things: Welcome to the Hellfire Club 2024

item.name: Blue Silk Handkerchief
item.text: Source: Stranger Things: Welcome to the Hellfire Club 2024

item.name: Pouch [5.5e]
item.text:
A Pouch holds up to 6 pounds within one-fifth of a cubic foot.
Source: Player's Handbook 2024 p. 228

item.name: Gold Piece (GP) [5.5e]
item.text:
Characters often find coins on their adventures and can spend those coins in shops, inns, and other businesses. Coins come in different denominations based on the relative worth of t heir material. The Coin Values table lists coins and how much they're worth relative to the Gold Piece, which is the game's main coin. For example, 100 Copper Pieces are worth 1 Gold Piece. A coin weighs about a third of an ounce, so fifty coins weigh a pound.
Coin | Value in GP Copper Piece (CP) | 1/100 Silver Piece (SP) | 1/10 Electrum Piece (EP) | 1/2 Gold Piece (GP) | 1 Platinum Piece (PP) | 10
Source: Player's Handbook 2024 p. 213

item.name: Missing Poster
item.text:
MISSING
19 Years of Age Brown Eyes Curly Black Hair Last Seen at RILLARD FAMILY FARM
Contact Rillard Sr. with ANY information
REWARD
Source: Stranger Things: Welcome to the Hellfire Club 2024

item.name: Poison-Soaked Kukri [5.5e]
item.text:
You can take a Bonus Action to magically coat the blade of this Dagger with poison. The poison remains for 1 minute or until an attack using this weapon hits a creature. That creature must succeed on a DC 13 Constitution saving throw or take 2d8 Poison damage and have the Poisoned condition for 1 minute. This Bonus Action can't be used again this way until the next dawn.
Finesse. When making an attack with a Finesse weapon, use your choice of your Strength or Dexterity modifier for the attack and damage rolls. You must use the same modifier for both rolls.
Light. When you take the Attack action on your turn and. attack with a Light weapon, you can make one extra attack as a Bonus Action later on the same turn. That extra attack must be made with a different Light weapon, and you don't add your ability modifier to the extra attack's damage unless that modifier is negative. For example, you can attack with a Shortsword in one hand and a Dagger in the other using the Attack action and a Bonus Action, but you don't add your Strength or Dexterity modifier to the damage roll of the Bonus Action unless that modifier is negative.
Thrown. If a weapon has the Thrown property, you can throw the weapon to make a ranged attack, and you can draw that weapon as part of the attack. If the weapon is a Melee weapon, use the same ability modifier for the attack and damage rolls that you use for a melee attack with that weapon.
Nick (Mastery). When you make the extra attack of the Light property, you can make it as part of the Attack action in· stead of as a Bonus Action. You can make this extra attack only once per turn.
Source: Stranger Things: Welcome to the Hellfire Club 2024

item.name: Gold Piece (GP) [5.5e]
item.text:
Characters often find coins on their adventures and can spend those coins in shops, inns, and other businesses. Coins come in different denominations based on the relative worth of t heir material. The Coin Values table lists coins and how much they're worth relative to the Gold Piece, which is the game's main coin. For example, 100 Copper Pieces are worth 1 Gold Piece. A coin weighs about a third of an ounce, so fifty coins weigh a pound.
Coin | Value in GP Copper Piece (CP) | 1/100 Silver Piece (SP) | 1/10 Electrum Piece (EP) | 1/2 Gold Piece (GP) | 1 Platinum Piece (PP) | 10
Source: Player's Handbook 2024 p. 213

note.name: Adventure Info
note.text:
Scream of the Crop is an adventure for Level 2 Characters.
This adventure is designed to fill one or two sessions of play.
If you aren't using the Map of Greyhawkins, it can take place near any agrarian settlement.

note.name: Key Plot Points
note.text:
This information is key to the adventure's story:
Plot Point 1. Rillard Senior, a local farmer, hires the characters to find his missing granddaughter, Rilly June, and investigate a string of unusual events around his farm.
Plot Point 2. During their search, the characters stumble on a network of tunnels beneath Rillard Family Farm.
Plot Point 3. The characters follow the tunnels to the source of the trouble: a Gulthias tree, a wicked sapling spawned from the stake of a slain vampire. Along the way, the characters confront subterranean monsters and living plants corrupted by the Gulthias tree.
Plot Point 4. The characters find Rilly June and destroy the Gulthias tree and the demonic hounds that guard it.

note.name: DM Preparation
note.text:
Before running the adventure, prepare as follows:
Step 1. Read the "Adventure Background" section.
Step 2. Familiarize yourself with the Missing Person Poster Handout and the Farm Tunnels Map.
Step 3. Familiarize yourself with the creatures in this adventure, which are as follows:
1 Ankheg 1 Commoner 3 Demodogs 3 Needle Blights Rilly June 1 Scarecrow 2 Vine Blight Tanglers 2 Violet Fungi
Step 4. Familiarize yourself with the magic items in this adventure, which are as follows:
+1 Dart Periapt of Health Poison-Soaked Kukri Potion of Resistance (Acid)

note.name: Player Assistance
note.text:
You can simplify the DM's role by having the other players handle tasks typically assigned to the DM. Before play begins, work with the other players to divide the following tasks among them:
Monster Wranglers. Whenever the characters encounter Hostile monsters, one or two players can run these monsters using the referenced stat blocks.
Rules Consultant. One player references the D&D Beyond Basic Rules as needed.

note.name: Adventure Background
note.text:
In decades past, the vampire Gunthar the Gluttonous terrorized the people of Goldenwheat. Gunthar and his three faithful hounds threatened to drink the farmers and their livestock dry. When all hope seemed lost, a group of brave heroes slew Gunthar in his crypt and plunged a stake into his heart. They left the crypt victorious, sealing the crypt behind them and leaving a stake buried in the vampire's corpse.
Over time, the blood-soaked stake warped and sprouted a Gulthias tree, a plant infused with evil essence. Malignance spreads from this tree, destroying crops, corrupting soil, and spawning awakened plants with a taste for blood. The tree's evil was contained for a time within Gunthar's crypt, and farms flourished in a land the vampire had filled with despair—until now.
An ankheg hungry for local livestock recently tunneled into Gunthar's crypt. The Gulthias tree's roots reached into the ankheg's tunnels, infecting them with blights and fungi and spoiling the crops above. The tree's vile tendrils even worked their way into the corpses of Gunthar's hounds. Returned to life and mutated by the tree, these bloodthirsty dogs loyally guard their master's resting place and destroy any who threaten the Gulthias tree.
A local farmer has reached out to the characters, hoping they can put an end to the blight affecting his crops and those of other nearby farms. If the disease continues to spread, the ensuing food shortage could prove disastrous.

note.name: Beginning the Adventure
note.text:
The bucolic hills of Goldenwheat hold fields of corn, seas of grain, and patches of plump pumpkins. Located along a prominent trail in southern Greyhawkins, these fertile farms regularly supply the region with bountiful harvests. At least one farm, however, has been struggling recently.
A brutal blight has struck Rillard Family Farm. For years, Rillard Senior, his granddaughter, and their hired hands have been a reliable source of wheat, producing hundreds of bushels each fall. If the blight isn't dealt with soon, some towns might go without bread this winter.
The adventure begins in Goldenwheat and assumes the characters have already accepted this heroic undertaking. Read or paraphrase the following text to begin the adventure:
"As you crest the hill into Goldenwheat, a pathetic sight greets you: a barren stretch of wilted gray stalks, collapsed gourds, and cracked earth. A stale breeze sweeps over the farms, carrying the stench of decay.
Near the road, a sad-looking scarecrow slumps on its stand, its rotting pumpkin head hung low and swarming with flies. Ahead, a wooden sign swings from a crooked post. It reads "Rillard Family Farm." Further up the road, a small farmhouse sits among the barren fields."
If the characters disturb the scarecrow, it falls apart. Otherwise, when a character next looks toward it, the scarecrow has mysteriously vanished.

note.name: Eddy Note
note.text: Foreshadowing is one of the most important skills in your DM toolbox, but players can throw you for a loop. The scarecrow the characters pass here is supposed to be the one they encounter later in the tunnels beneath the farm, but if they **** with it, you'll have to pretend that was a totally different scarecrow. Hint at the scarecrow along the road without making it seem too interesting or important. Then wait for the juicy reveal.

note.name: Arriving at Rillard Family Farm
note.text:
Rillard Family Farm grows crops and raises livestock as part of the Goldenwheat farming collective. When the characters arrive at the farmhouse, read or paraphrase the following text:
"The Rillard farmhouse is a humble two-story home with a wraparound porch. An elderly halfling wearing a straw hat sits in a rocking chair on the porch, staring at the blighted fields.
The chair creaks as the halfling rises slowly. He looks you over while scratching his bushy sideburns and nods. "Well met," he says.
"Y'all look like a band of capable folks. I'm Rillard Senior, but you can call me Ol' Rill. I hate to admit it, but my farm and my family are in a heap of trouble. I'm hoping y'all can help us out. These old bones ain't made for adventuring." "
Rillard (Small, Neutral Good Commoner) is a lifelong farmer who periodically hires adventurers to help around his farm and ward off varmints. He is Friendly toward the characters and treats them with respect. After a brief introduction, Rillard explains that strange happenings have befallen his farm. He shares the following details:
Blighted Crops. The crops on the farm are dying, even after magical intervention by a traveling mage. Rillard doesn't know the blight's cause.
Missing Person. Rillard's granddaughter, Rilly June, is missing. Rillard says she's a tough cookie who can hold her own even after a hard day's work on the farm, but he's starting to worry. He hasn't seen her in two days.
Stolen Scarecrows. Meddlesome whippersnappers have been stealing Rillard's scarecrows, each of which has a pumpkin for a head. If the characters mention they saw one along the road (see "Beginning the Adventure"), Rillard remarks that one of his scarecrows was plucked from that very spot a few days ago, but he hadn't replaced it yet. He jokes that the scarecrow might have "up and run off on its own."
Vanishing Livestock. Someone has been poaching Rillard's heifers. He locks the barn every night, but one cow disappears every other night.

note.name: Rillard's Request
note.text:
Rillard asks the characters to find his granddaughter and investigate the unusual events around his farm. He offers them 200 GP to find Rilly June and unearth the source of the blight.
If the characters accept, Rillard provides them with a sketch of Rilly June that he was planning to give to the authorities. Show your players the Missing Person Poster. Rillard also loans the party his great granddaddy's Poison-Soaked Kukri, hoping it will aid them in their exploration.

note.name: Story Time
note.text:
If the characters accept Rillard's quest, he thanks them kindly and says they remind him of the brave heroes who saved these lands long ago. Rillard then briefly recounts the story of the vampire Gunthar the Gluttonous (as detailed in this adventure's background), though he knows nothing of the ankheg tunnels or the Gulthias tree beneath his farm.
Rillard adds that some say Gunthar cursed these farmlands with his last breath, while others say the vampire haunts these lands still. The old farmer believes the story is simply used to scare young children so they return home before nightfall.

note.name: Exploring the Farm
note.text:
The characters can investigate any of the events Rillard observed, each of which leads them to one of the tunnels beneath the farm (detailed in the following section). Rillard can point them in the right direction as follows:
Blighted Crops. If the characters go into the fields to investigate the crops, they stumble on a tunnel entrance hidden behind a rotten bale of wheat. The tunnel slopes down to area T1.
Missing Person. If the characters search the farmhouse for clues to Rilly June's whereabouts, they happen on several loose floorboards in the basement that, when pried up, reveal an earthen tunnel. The tunnel descends to area T6.
Stolen Scarecrow. If the characters return to where they last saw the scarecrow, they quickly find a trail of straw that leads to a hole in the ground. It leads to area T3.
Vanishing Livestock. If the characters explore the barn, they find a hole beneath a partially collapsed hay pile. It leads to area T4.

note.name: Eddy Note
note.text: Listen, I get it. Everyone wants to adopt a friendly NPC to tag along on a quest—hell, devious players even use them as dungeon cannon fodder. Point is, this is where you can put your foot down, and say, "Nope, Ol' Rill ain't goin' with you." He's like eighty years old.

note.name: Farm Tunnels
note.text:
Beneath Rillard Family Farm lies a network of tunnels dug by a ravenous ankheg. The ankheg inadvertently burrowed into the sealed crypt of Gunthar the Gluttonous, unleashing the roots of the Gulthias tree that grows there. The tree's roots have spread rapidly from the dead vampire's resting place, overtaking the tunnels and releasing a rash of blights to wreak havoc on the surrounding farms.
When the characters enter the tunnels, the Player Characters' starting location on the Farm Tunnels Map is based on which event they investigated (see "Exploring the Farm"). Don't reveal the locations of the tunnels' other contents at the outset, as they aren't yet visible to the characters.

note.name: Tunnel Features
note.text:
Unless otherwise noted, the tunnels have the following features:
Ceilings. The ceilings are 10 feet high.
Lighting. The tunnels are unlit. Creatures rely on Darkvision, Blindsight, or Tremorsense to see. Area descriptions assume the characters have a light source or other means of seeing in Darkness.
Roots and Vines. Writhing, ink-black roots and vines pervade the tunnels. Areas covered in roots (as denoted on the map) are Difficult Terrain. As a Study action, a character can inspect the plants and make a DC 13 Intelligence (Arcana) check; a successful check reveals the vegetation is corrupted by a malevolent supernatural force.

note.name: Conclusion
note.text:
When the characters return to the farmhouse, Rillard awaits them on the front porch. How he greets them depends on whether his granddaughter made it home safely.
If the characters rescued Rilly June from the tunnels beneath the farm, Rillard is elated to see them. Read or paraphrase the following text:
"As you approach the farmhouse, Rillard leaps from his rocking chair. "Well yank my sideburns and call me cornbread. It turns out real heroes do still exist!" he exclaims.
Rillard tips his hat. "You have done my family and this farm an immeasurable kindness. Why don't y'all come on in and rest a while? I've got pumpkin soup on the stove. You've earned it." "
Rillard invites the characters to stay the night and enjoy a home-cooked meal. He pays them before they set out on their next adventure. Rilly June and her grandfather invite the characters to revisit the farm any time, providing free, comfortable lodgings and a hot meal whenever the characters stop in.
If the characters return without Rilly June, read or paraphrase the following text:
As you approach the farmhouse, you spot Rillard in the same old rocking chair. He hasn't moved since you left.
Rillard hangs his head in disappointment. "Alone, I see," he mutters. "I guess heroes just ain't what they used to be."
If Rilly June met her end in the tunnels beneath the farm, Rillard's reward is the same, but he pays the characters solemnly and doesn't invite them to stay. Otherwise, Rillard urges the characters to return to the tunnels and rescue his granddaughter.

note.name: Eddy Note
note.text: If Rilly June accompanies the characters on their quest to end the blight, you can either run Rilly June yourself or give her Stat Block to another player. NPCs like her are a great way to deliver in-world hints or lore. Try not to get them killed.

note.name: Rillard's Reward
note.text: Rillard pays the characters 200 GP as promised. He also lets the characters keep his great grandaddy's Poison-Soaked Kukri.

note.name: Character Advancement
note.text: After the characters receive Rillard's reward, everyone in the party gains 1 level. Direct each player to record any treasure they found over the course of this adventure.

note.name: Eddy Note
note.text: Reward players who think outside of the box. It's called the Rule of Cool, a.k.a. the Don't-Be-A-Square Rule. Take the Gulthias tree, for example. If the characters want to cast a spell to break the roots, conduct a ritual to break the tree's magic, or lure the ankheg into melting it with acid—embrace their creativity! This is your table now, and you make the rules. Don't let them get in the way of a good time. As long as everyone is having fun, there's no wrong way to play D&D.

note.name: Read
note.text: A boot-shaped wooden sign near the entrance of this shop reads "Suresoles Boots Shoes."

note.name: Info
note.text:
The proprietor of this cobbler's shop is a superstitious dwarf named Nina Suresoles (Medium, Chaotic Good Commoner). Her passion for finely crafted footwear is matched only by her interest in ghosts and the afterlife.
When the characters enter, read or paraphrase the following text:
"The smell of leather fills this warm shop, whose walls are lined with shelves displaying boots and shoes of every size. A young dwarf with thick braids emerges from the back room to greet you. "Customers! Come on in," she says. "I've got boots and shoes for sale; which'll it be?" "
Nina is Friendly toward the characters and welcomes them into her shop regardless of whether they present the writ of authority. She offers to sell the characters boots or shoes for 1 GP per pair.
If the characters question Nina about the murders, her sunny disposition turns solemn. She asks them to follow her into the workshop (area 01b).

monster.label: Swarm of Corrupted Rats [5.5e]
monster.name: Swarm of Corrupted Rats [5.5e]
monster.conditionImmune: Charmed, Frightened, Grappled, Paralyzed, Petrified, Prone, Restrained, Stunned

trait.name: Source
trait.text: Stranger Things: Welcome to the Hellfire Club

trait.name: Death Burst
trait.text: The rat explodes when it dies. Constitution Saving Throw: DC 10, each creature in a 10-foot Emanation originating from the rat. Failure: 2d4 Acid Damage. Success: Half damage.

trait.name: Swarm
trait.text: The swarm can occupy another creature's space and vice versa, and the swarm can move through any opening large enough for a Tiny rat. The swarm can't regain Hit Points.

action.name: Bite
action.text: Melee Attack Roll: +4, Reach 5 ft. Hit: 6 (2d4 + 2) Piercing damage or 1d4 + 2 Piercing damage if the swarm is Bloodied.

note.name: Read
note.text: Damaged shoes are piled in the center of this workshop. Cobbler's tools, soles, and leather scraps hang from the walls, and a pristine pair of fancy red boots rests on a workbench.

note.name: DM Secret!
note.text: Don't reveal the Swarm of Corrupted Rats yet. These monsters are hiding.

note.name: Info
note.text: The pile of damaged shoes is 3 feet tall and Difficult Terrain. Three Swarms of Corrupted Rats hide in the shoe pile. If a character moves within 5 feet of the pile or otherwise disturbs it, the Hostile rats erupt from the pile and begin combat. If Nina is present, she shrieks and takes refuge in the storefront (area 01a); the rats ignore her.

note.name: Questioning Nina
note.text:
Although she speaks in hushed tones, Nina answers all questions honestly and knows the following information:
Fancy Boots. The eye-catching red boots on Nina's workbench in this area were commissioned by an adventurer named Korex as part of a stage outfit. When Korex couldn't pay for the boots, the cobbler kept them. Nina hasn't seen Korex since.
Haunted Shoes. Nina believes the shoes in her workshop are haunted by the murderer's victims. On the night of each incident, Nina saw the shoes move on their own, cryptically adding that "damaged souls move damaged soles."
Rats. Rats are a regular sight at the docks, but they've been acting strange lately. Customers have complained about rats gnawing at their shoes, which is why Nina has a heap awaiting repair.

note.name: Treasure
note.text: If the characters deal with the rats, Nina gives the characters the boots she made for Korex. The boots are made of red leather encrusted with glitter and are worth 20 GP.

item.name: Korex's Fancy Stage Boots
item.text:
The boots are made of red leather encrusted with glitter.
Source: Stranger Things: Welcome to the Hellfire Club 2024

note.name: Read
note.text:
The door to this home is ajar. When the characters approach, read or paraphrase the following text:
"A frazzled, grandmotherly gnome peers at you suspiciously from behind the door of this home. An artfully crafted wooden sign above reads "Bibble's Carpentry." The gnome calls out in a shaky voice. "It's not safe out there. Haven't you heard? There's a curfew in place; no one's allowed outside." "

note.name: Info
note.text:
The gnome is a carpenter named Bibble Sunspot (Small Commoner). She is Indifferent toward the characters and cautiously addresses them from behind the partially opened door. Bibble quickly shuts and locks the door if the characters try to enter her home without her permission.
A character can try to convince Bibble to let them in and make a DC 15 Charisma (Persuasion) check. On a successful check, Bibble's attitude improves to Friendly, and she welcomes them in. On a failed check, Bibble closes and locks the door. If the characters present their writ of authority, Bibble reluctantly invites them into her home.
When the characters enter, read or paraphrase the following text:
"The windows in this cozy home are covered with green curtains that block out natural light. Flickering candles illuminate the wood furniture here, all of which is covered in gnaw marks. Muffled screeches frequently erupt from behind a door on the other side of the room."
The few candles leave most of this sitting room in Dim Light.
Bibble crafted all the furniture here. Rats have been chewing on the furniture at night, damaging both priceless heirlooms and pieces for sale. The screeching comes from Bibble's workshop (area 02b), where she recently trapped a big rat.

note.name: Questioning Bibble
note.text:
f questioned, Bibble becomes nervous and lies. She claims she didn't see or hear anything. A character who doubts Bibble's claims can make a DC 12 Wisdom (Insight) check; a successful check reveals that she's lying.
As an Influence action, a character can try to pry more information out of Bibble and make a DC 12 Charisma (Persuasion) check. If they saw through Bibble's lie, the check succeeds automatically. On a successful check, Bibble reveals what she knows (detailed below). On a failed check, she refuses to speak to them further and asks them to leave.
Bibble knows the following information:
Murder Music. On the nights of the murders, Bibble heard eerie music coming from the docks. Bibble thinks the music came from a flute.
Korex. The only musician Bibble remembers in Oakshore was a washed-up adventurer who wore out his welcome long ago. He used to perform at the local tavern (area 04).
Rats. Bibble has seen some big rats in Oakshore, and they sometimes find their way into her home. Last night, she trapped "a real whopper" in her workshop (area 02b). Bibble offers to pay the characters if they dispose of the rat.

monster.label: Giant Corrupted Rat [5.5e]
monster.name: Giant Corrupted Rat [5.5e]

trait.name: Source
trait.text: Stranger Things: Welcome to the Hellfire Club

trait.name: Death Burst
trait.text: The rat explodes when it dies. Constitution Saving Throw DC 11, each creature in a 5-foot Emanation originating from the rat. Failure: 5 (2d4) Acid damage. Success: Half damage.

action.name: Bite
action.text: Melee Attack Roll: +5, Reach 5 ft. Hit: 6 (1d6 + 3) Piercing damage plus 5 (2d4) Acid damage.

monster.text: The rats of Elmshire sometimes grow to unusual proportions. Though frightening, these dog-size rodents were little more than a local nuisance until an ex-adventurer's magic transformed them into feral terrors. Caustic drool drips from their chattering incisors, and glowing pustules pockmark their bloated, ghastly hides.

note.name: Read
note.text:
The door to this workshop is locked (see "Oakshore Docks Features"). Bibble carries the key and can unlock the door for the characters. When they enter, read or paraphrase the following:
"The screeching stops as you enter this workshop, which contains carpentry tools and unfinished furniture. In the center of the room, a giant, mangy rat gnaws on a hunk of splintered wood."

note.name: Info
note.text: Dim Light fills the workshop. A Giant Corrupted Rat has been chewing on in-progress furniture since Bibble trapped it here. The rat is Hostile toward all other creatures.

note.name: Treasure
note.text: If the characters deal with the rat in the workshop, Bibble rewards each character with 1 GP and a stale butterscotch.

item.name: Gold Piece (GP) [5.5e]
item.text:
Characters often find coins on their adventures and can spend those coins in shops, inns, and other businesses. Coins come in different denominations based on the relative worth of their material. The Coin Values table lists coins and how much they're worth relative to the Gold Piece, which is the game's main coin. For example, 100 Copper Pieces are worth 1 Gold Piece. A coin weighs about a third of an ounce, so fifty coins weigh a pound.
Coin | Value in GP Copper Piece (CP) | 1/100 Silver Piece (SP) | 1/10 Electrum Piece (EP) | 1/2 Gold Piece (GP) | 1 Platinum Piece (PP) | 10
Source: Player's Handbook 2024 p. 213

item.name: Stale Butterscotch
item.text: Source: Stranger Things: Welcome to the Hellfire Club 2024

note.name: Read
note.text: An elegant sign hangs above the door of this humble shop. It reads "Sunderson's Cloaks." Below the sign, a note reads: "Closed until the murders cease—unless you're buying cloaks."

note.name: DM Secret!
note.text: Although Wexley uses the Cultist of Demogorgon stat block, represent him on maps using a Commoner token. If his secret is revealed, swap that token for the Cultist of Demogorgon token.

note.name: Info
note.text:
The proprietor of this tailor shop is an arrogant elf named Wexley Sunderson. When he isn't secretly worshiping Demogorgon, Prince of Demons, Wexley designs cloaks for Oakshore's well-to-do citizens.
When the characters enter, read or paraphrase:
"This sparsely appointed tailor's shop contains a bookcase, textile crates, and cloaks on display. A haughty-looking elf stands amid them, brandishing a large pair of shears. He clears his throat."
"I assume you can read. So, you're here either to kill me or to buy a cloak. Which is it?"
Wexley (Medium, Chaotic Evil Commoner (Stranger Things)) is initially suspicious but Indifferent toward the characters. If the characters reply that they are here to kill him or if they try to enter his secret room (area 03b), Wexley becomes Hostile and starts combat.

note.name: Secret Door
note.text: The bookcase is a secret door to area 03b. As a Study action, a character can examine the bookshelf and make a DC 15 Intelligence (Investigation) check; a successful check reveals an occult tome, which, if pulled, opens the secret door.

note.name: Questioning Wexley
note.text:
Wexley answers the characters' questions only if they first buy one of his cloaks, which cost 10 GP each. He doesn't respect the writ of authority and laughs if they present it.
Wexley worries the characters might find his secret room. A character who takes the Search action to observe Wexley and succeeds on a DC 12 Wisdom (Insight) check sees him glance at the bookcase.
Wexley knows the following information:
Rats. An unusual number of customers have needed their clothes repaired lately. The fabric often looks as if it has been chewed by rodents.
Rumors. The local tavern, Pints Ahoy (area 04), is a good source of juicy gossip.

trait.name: Source
trait.text: Stranger Things: Welcome to the Hellfire Club 2024

action.name: Bite
action.text: Melee Attack Roll: +5 (with Advantage if the target is Grappled by a Demobat), reach 5 ft. Hit: 10 (2d6 + 3) Piercing damage.

action.name: Tentacles
action.text: Melee Attack Roll: +5, reach 5 ft. Hit: 7 (1d8 + 3) Slashing damage. If the target is a Medium or smaller creature, the target has the Grappled condition (escape DC 13) from all four tentacles.

trait.name: Source
trait.text: Stranger Things: Welcome to the Hellfire Club 2024

action.name: Bite
action.text: Melee Attack Roll: +5 (with Advantage if the target is Grappled by a Demobat), reach 5 ft. Hit: 10 (2d6 + 3) Piercing damage.

action.name: Tentacles
action.text: Melee Attack Roll: +5, reach 5 ft. Hit: 7 (1d8 + 3) Slashing damage. If the target is a Medium or smaller creature, the target has the Grappled condition (escape DC 13) from all four tentacles.

trait.name: Source
trait.text: Stranger Things: Welcome to the Hellfire Club 2024

action.name: Bite
action.text: Melee Attack Roll: +5 (with Advantage if the target is Grappled by a Demobat), reach 5 ft. Hit: 10 (2d6 + 3) Piercing damage.

action.name: Tentacles
action.text: Melee Attack Roll: +5, reach 5 ft. Hit: 7 (1d8 + 3) Slashing damage. If the target is a Medium or smaller creature, the target has the Grappled condition (escape DC 13) from all four tentacles.

note.name: Read
note.text: A wicked symbol is painted on the floor of this secret room. Cultists' robes and other garments hang on racks along one wall.

note.name: Info
note.text:
As a Study action, a character can inspect the symbol and make a DC 13 Intelligence (Religion) check; a successful check reveals it to be a ritual circle that cultists of Demogorgon use to summon demons.
If Wexley dies, three Demobats burst from the symbol. The Hostile demons appear in three unoccupied spaces nearest the symbol and begin combat when they detect the characters.

note.name: Treasure
note.text: A Cloak of Billowing hangs on the wall.

item.name: Cloak of Billowing [5.5e]
item.text:
Rarity: Common
While wearing this cloak, you can take a Bonus Action to make it billow dramatically for 1 minute.
Source: Dungeon Master's Guide 2024 p. 244

note.name: Read
note.text: This miserable-looking establishment has seen better days. A wooden anchor hangs above the door by a single frayed rope. It reads "Pints Ahoy!" Instrumental music plays within.

note.name: Info
note.text:
Even in these trying times, the tavern remains open. When the characters enter, read or paraphrase the following text:
"A lively sea chantey fills this ramshackle tavern, whose walls are festooned with nautical decor. A lanky human man with a voluminous pompadour prances about the room, playing an accordion. He wears a sailor's uniform, as does the woman sitting tiredly behind the bar. The tavern is otherwise empty.
"Ahoy, adventurers!" says the frolicking sailor. "Would you like to set sail on an ocean of ale with me? My name is Captain Jeeves Whalington, and I'll be your seafaring guide on this libationary voyage. This is my first mate, Jay Le'Buck. To what sherry shore shall we set sail to first?" "
Jay and Jeeves greet the characters warmly and are Friendly toward their only customers. If the characters present the writ of authority, Jeeves offers them a free glass of milk or pint of ale (drinker's choice). He insists they call him Captain Whalington.

note.name: Questioning Jay and Jeeves
note.text:
Jeeves welcomes any conversation, including questions about recent events. As the co-owner of the local drinking hole, he knows a little about every dockside resident. Jay chimes in only to correct her brother or deride him with the occasional sarcastic remark.
Jay and Jeeves know the following information:
Carpenter. Bibble Sunspot, the carpenter (see area 02a), has been acting strange lately. She refuses to let anyone into her home anymore. Cobbler. Nina Suresoles, the cobbler (see areas 01a and 01b), recently crafted a pair of fantastic boots for Korex, a local musician who used to perform at Pints Ahoy.
Herbalist. Lampro Capnos, the herbalist (see area 05), nearly went out of business before the murderers, but they seem to be doing fine now.
Musician. Jeeves hired Korex to perform in the tavern for a time, but he eventually let Korex go due to the musician's bad manners and poor hygiene. Korex used to live just down the street (see area 07), but that house has since been abandoned.
Tailor. Wexley Sunderson, the tailor (see area 03), has some titillating titles on his bookshelf. Jay thinks the tailor has an embarrassing secret.

note.name: Treasure
note.text: The tavern owners keep 55 GP in an iron lockbox behind the bar. As a Utilize action, a character can try to pick the lock using Thieves' Tools; a successful DC 15 Dexterity (Sleight of Hand) check opens it. Jay carries the key to the lockbox, but both siblings become Hostile if they notice a character trying to pilfer their profits.

item.name: Gold Piece (GP) [5.5e]
item.text:
Characters often find coins on their adventures and can spend those coins in shops, inns, and other businesses. Coins come in different denominations based on the relative worth of their material. The Coin Values table lists coins and how much they're worth relative to the Gold Piece, which is the game's main coin. For example, 100 Copper Pieces are worth 1 Gold Piece. A coin weighs about a third of an ounce, so fifty coins weigh a pound.
Coin | Value in GP Copper Piece (CP) | 1/100 Silver Piece (SP) | 1/10 Electrum Piece (EP) | 1/2 Gold Piece (GP) | 1 Platinum Piece (PP) | 10
Source: Player's Handbook 2024 p. 213

note.name: Read
note.text: The front of this quaint shop is adorned with a bottle-shaped placard that reads "Lampro's Potions." The exterior is overgrown with lush, flowered vines.

note.name: Info
note.text:
The proprietor of this shop is an elf herbalist named Lampro Capnos. When the characters enter, read or paraphrase the following text:
"Customers! Yes, customers!" a soothing voice calls from inside. As the door opens, a smiling elf in a leather apron beckons you into a jungle-like room. An overwhelming odor of strong perfumes mingles with the smell of fresh dirt. A hammock hangs between two potted citrus trees behind a counter brimming with jarred powders, petals, and bulbs.
Lampro (Medium Commoner) is Indifferent toward the characters. After introducing themself, Lampro asks whether the characters have come to purchase plants or potions.
The plants that fill the shop range from fragrant floral arrangements to important alchemical ingredients. A character who inspects Lampro's plants and succeeds on a DC 13 Intelligence (Nature) check recognizes that many of them have medicinal properties.

note.name: Potions for Sale
note.text: In addition to common house plants and floral arrangements, Lampro sells Potions of Healing for 50 GP each. The herbalist has 1d6 vials amid the foliage of a large plant. A character who carefully surveys the shop's interior and succeeds on a DC 18 Wisdom (Perception) check notices the potions. Characters who witnessed Lampro retrieve a potion automatically succeed on this check.

note.name: Questioning Lampro
note.text:
Fearing gossip could hurt their struggling business, Lampro answers the party's questions only if a character presents the writ of authority.
Lampro knows the following information:
Bad Business. Even before the murders started, Oakshore's dockside businesses have struggled. Lampro says the cobbler (see area 01a) was stiffed by a customer who ordered a fancy pair of boots. However, the uptick of crime has resulted in more customers coming to Lampro seeking precautionary Potions of Healing. Lampro feels guilty for the boost in sales.
Missing Cat. Lampro's pet cat, Mr. Whiskers, recently escaped the shop, but due to the curfew, Lampro hasn't been able to look for him. Lampro offers the characters a protective potion if they find Mr. Whiskers. The cat has a bright orange coat and likes to hunt rats in the alley (area 06).
Musician. Lampro's old next-door neighbor was a musician and former adventurer. He used to play the most beautiful songs on his pan flute in the evenings, but his house is now abandoned (see areas 07a and 07b). Some nights, Lampro swears they can still hear him playing.

note.name: Treasure
note.text: After learning Mr. Whiskers's grisly fate (detailed in area 06), the characters can report the bad news to Lampro. Alternatively, a character who fabricates a more pleasant outcome for Mr. Whiskers and succeeds on a DC 15 Charisma (Deception) check convinces Lampro that the story is true. Either way, Lampro rewards the characters as promised with a Potion of Resistance (Acid).

item.name: Potion of Acid Resistance [5.5e]
item.text:
Rarity: Uncommon
When you drink this potion, you have Resistance to Acid damage for 1 hour.
Source: Dungeon Master's Guide 2024 p. 289

monster.label: Giant Corrupted Rat [5.5e] 1
monster.name: Giant Corrupted Rat [5.5e]

trait.name: Source
trait.text: Stranger Things: Welcome to the Hellfire Club

trait.name: Death Burst
trait.text: The rat explodes when it dies. Constitution Saving Throw DC 11, each creature in a 5-foot Emanation originating from the rat. Failure: 5 (2d4) Acid damage. Success: Half damage.

action.name: Bite
action.text: Melee Attack Roll: +5, Reach 5 ft. Hit: 6 (1d6 + 3) Piercing damage plus 5 (2d4) Acid damage.

monster.text: The rats of Elmshire sometimes grow to unusual proportions. Though frightening, these dog-size rodents were little more than a local nuisance until an ex-adventurer's magic transformed them into feral terrors. Caustic drool drips from their chattering incisors, and glowing pustules pockmark their bloated, ghastly hides.

note.name: Read
note.text: The stones of this dreary alley are flecked with blood. Foul drainage trickles down one side of the alleyway into a sewer grate, while lidded trash cans line the opposite wall. A disgusting squelching noise echoes inside one of the bins.

note.name: Info
note.text: Inside the largest of the trash cans are two Giant Corrupted Rats feeding on the carcass of Mr. Whiskers, Lampro's missing cat (see area 05). The rats are Hostile toward the characters but are preoccupied with their meal. If a character opens any of the cans or otherwise disturbs the rats, the rats spring from the container and begin combat.

note.name: Sewer Grate
note.text:
The sewer grate leads to a system of sewers and flood channels under Oakshore. At night, Korex emerges from the grate to terrorize the dockside neighborhood.
The sewers and their contents are beyond the scope of this adventure, but you can flesh them out as you see fit if the characters wish to extend their investigation to the tunnels below.

note.name: Trash Cans
note.text: The trash cans contain food scraps and other dockside refuse. Inside the largest trash can are the remains of Mr. Whiskers. The characters can identify Mr. Whiskers by the name on his collar, which they can bring to Lampro at the herbalist's shop (area 05) as proof of the cat's demise.

item.name: Mr. Whiskers Collar
item.text: Source: Stranger Things: Welcome to the Hellfire Club 2024

note.name: Read
note.text: This house is in shambles. A torn eviction notice is nailed to the front door, whose once-white paint is now yellowed and peeling. The windows are thick with grime, and a foul odor wafts from within.

note.name: Info
note.text:
Korex was evicted from this house after running afoul of his creditors and the people of Oakshore. Although the house appears abandoned, Korex has slowly been moving back into his old quarters.
The front door is locked (see "Oakshore Docks Features"). Dirty windows obscure the home's interior.
When the characters enter the house, read or paraphrase the following text:
"Rat droppings and clumps of mangy fur litter the floor of this sparsely furnished living area, which reeks of sewage. A soiled mattress lies near an old couch, and a battered lute hangs on the wall."
When Korex isn't serenading sewer rats or stalking the docks for his next victim, he hides here, often joined by his rat thralls. The lute on the wall smells like an outhouse and always sounds out of tune.
The rats in area 07b investigate any loud noises in this area.

note.name: Eddy Note
note.text:
If your players are anything like mine, they might be stupid and use the Speaking Stone early. If that happens, just have Fillius show up when you need him to.
********.

item.name: Lute [5.5e]
item.text:
Instrument
Ability: Charisma Utilize: Play a known tune (DC 10), or improvise a song (DC 15)
Source: Player's Handbook 2024 p. 221

note.name: Read
note.text: A draft flows into this mostly empty bedroom through a broken window. Inside, three monstrous rats gnaw on the remains of a pelican. The rats snarl and growl as they fight over the scraps. Past the rats, a chest sits near the wall.

note.name: Info
note.text: The three Giant Corrupted Rats are Hostile toward the characters and fight them on sight.

note.name: Treasure
note.text: Korex stores his spoils in the chest, which contains 75 GP, a gold bracelet worth 30 GP, a silk handkerchief embroidered with silver thread worth 10 GP, and Holly's Handy Haversack.

item.name: Gold Piece (GP) [5.5e]
item.text:
Characters often find coins on their adventures and can spend those coins in shops, inns, and other businesses. Coins come in different denominations based on the relative worth of their material. The Coin Values table lists coins and how much they're worth relative to the Gold Piece, which is the game's main coin. For example, 100 Copper Pieces are worth 1 Gold Piece. A coin weighs about a third of an ounce, so fifty coins weigh a pound.
Coin | Value in GP Copper Piece (CP) | 1/100 Silver Piece (SP) | 1/10 Electrum Piece (EP) | 1/2 Gold Piece (GP) | 1 Platinum Piece (PP) | 10
Source: Player's Handbook 2024 p. 213

item.name: Gold Bracelet
item.text: Source: Stranger Things: Welcome to the Hellfire Club 2024

item.name: Silk handkerchief
item.text: Stranger Things: Welcome to the Hellfire Club 2024

item.name: Holly's Handy Haversack [5.5e]
item.text:
Rarity: Uncommon
This backpack has a central pouch and two side pouches, each of which is an extradimensional space. Each side pouch can hold up to 200 pounds of material, not exceeding a volume of 25 cubic feet. The central pouch can hold up to 500 pounds of material, not exceeding a volume of 64 cubic feet. The haversack always weighs 5 pounds, regardless of its contents. Retrieving an item from the haversack requires a Utilize action or a Bonus Action (your choice). When you reach into the haversack for a specific item, the item is always magically on top. If any of its pouches is overloaded, pierced, or torn, the haversack ruptures and is destroyed. If the haversack is destroyed, its contents are lost forever, although an Artifact always turns up again somewhere. If the haversack is turned inside out, its contents spill forth unharmed, and the haversack must be put right before it can be used again. Each pouch of the haversack holds enough air for 10 minutes of breathing, divided by the number of breathing creatures inside. Placing the haversack inside an extradimensional space created by a Bag of Holding, Portable Hole, or similar item instantly destroys both items and opens a gate to the Astral Plane. The gate originates where the one item was placed inside the other. Any creature within 10 feet of the gate and not behind Total Cover is sucked through it and deposited in a random location on the Astral Plane. The gate then closes. The gate is one-way only and can't be reopened.
Source: Stranger Things: Welcome to the Hellfire Club 2024

note.name: Read
note.text: Waves crash against the pillars of this bloodstained pier. A loading crane looms over a single rowboat bobbing on the water.

note.name: Info
note.text:
The docks rest 5 feet below the street line and are accessible by two flights of stone stairs.
Boat. A single rowboat is moored at the docks. Inside are a metal pail, a fishing rod, and a burlap satchel that contains moldy cheese and stale bread.
Crane. Affixed to the dock is a sturdy crane fitted with a weighted net. As a Utilize action, a creature within 5 feet of the crane can drop the net on a Medium or smaller creature it can see within 20 feet of the crane. The target must succeed on a DC 15 Dexterity saving throw or have the Restrained condition until it escapes.
To escape the net, the target or a creature within 5 feet of it must take an action to make a DC 10 Strength (Athletics) check, freeing the Restrained creature on a successful check. Destroying the net (AC 10; 5 HP; Immunity to Bludgeoning, Poison, and Psychic damage) also frees the target, ending the effect.

trait.name: Source
trait.text: Stranger Things: Welcome to the Hellfire Club

trait.name: Gear
trait.text: Pipes of Pestilence

trait.name: Sewer Speech
trait.text: Korex can comprehend and verbally communicate with Monstrosities.

action.name: Rancid Knife
action.text: Melee Attack Roll: +5, reach 5 ft. Hit: 8 (2d4 + 3) Piercing damage plus 7 (2d6) Poison damage, and the target has the Poisoned condition until the start of Korex's next turn.

action.name: Entrancing Pipes
action.text: Wisdom Saving Throw: DC 12, one creature Korex can see within 120 feet. Failure: 14 (4d6) Psychic damage, and the target has the Charmed condition until the start of Korex's next turn. Success: Half damage only.

action.name: Skitter (bonus action)
action.text: Korex takes the Dash or Disengage action.

trait.name: Death Burst
trait.text: The rat explodes when it dies. Constitution Saving Throw: DC 10, each creature in a 10-foot Emanation originating from the rat. Failure: 2d4 Acid Damage. Success: Half damage.

trait.name: Swarm
trait.text: The swarm can occupy another creature's space and vice versa, and the swarm can move through any opening large enough for a Tiny rat. The swarm can't regain Hit Points.

action.name: Bite
action.text: Melee Attack Roll: +4, Reach 5 ft. Hit: 6 (2d4 + 2) Piercing damage or 1d4 + 2 Piercing damage if the swarm is Bloodied.

monster.label: Swarm of Corrupted Rats [5.5e] 2
monster.name: Swarm of Corrupted Rats [5.5e]
monster.conditionImmune: Charmed, Frightened, Grappled, Paralyzed, Petrified, Prone, Restrained, Stunned

trait.name: Source
trait.text: Stranger Things: Welcome to the Hellfire Club

trait.name: Death Burst
trait.text: The rat explodes when it dies. Constitution Saving Throw: DC 10, each creature in a 10-foot Emanation originating from the rat. Failure: 2d4 Acid Damage. Success: Half damage.

trait.name: Swarm
trait.text: The swarm can occupy another creature's space and vice versa, and the swarm can move through any opening large enough for a Tiny rat. The swarm can't regain Hit Points.

action.name: Bite
action.text: Melee Attack Roll: +4, Reach 5 ft. Hit: 6 (2d4 + 2) Piercing damage or 1d4 + 2 Piercing damage if the swarm is Bloodied.

note.name: Info
note.text:
That evening, Korex emerges from the sewer grate in the alley (area 06), followed by two Swarms of Corrupted Rats. Korex and the swarms are Hostile toward all non-rat creatures. Korex and the swarms then roam the pier (area 08) before returning to the abandoned house (areas 07a and 07b), where they remain for the rest of the night before returning to the sewers.
When the characters confront Korex, read or paraphrase the following text:
"A wild-eyed elf wearing a tattered cloak dances down the street, playing a haunting melody on a pan flute. A flood of sickly rats trails behind him, skittering to the beat his tune. The music stops.
"Well, well, well! If it isn't a bunch of goody-goodies come to save the day. Oh well. More food for my rats." "
Korex and the swarms fight to the death.

note.name: Treasure
note.text: Korex carries the Pipes of Pestilence.

item.name: Pipes of Pestilence [5.5e]
item.text:
Rarity: Uncommon (requires Attunement)
If you play these pipes as a Magic action, you summon one Swarm of Corrupted Rats in an unoccupied space within 60 feet of yourself. The swarm is Friendly to you and your allies for as long as you continue to play the pipes each round as a Magic action. While Friendly, the swarm is under your control, obeys your commands, and takes no action other than to defend itself if you issue it no commands. Once your control ends, the swarm vanishes, and the pipes can't be used again until the next dawn.
Source: Stranger Things: Welcome to the Hellfire Club 2024

item.name: Oakshore Writ of Authority
item.text:
By the authority of Oakshore
This writ hereby authorizes the writ holder and their companions to conduct a thorough investigation on behalf of the Oakshore Guard. It excludes these investigators from any active curfews.
Residents may be subject to interrogation and are expected to comply with the investigation as carried out by the writ holder and their companions, to include permitting of entry into private residences or businesses outside normal hours. Suspicious activity might prompt a thorough search of any business or place of residence, as deemed necessary by the writ holder and their companions.
Residents must conform to all local ordinances and should treat the writ holder and their companions as extensions of the Oakshore Guard.
Signed,
Fillius Kellaghan Executive Officer
Source: Stranger Things: Welcome to the Hellfire Club 2024

item.name: Speaking Stones [5.5e]
item.text:
Rarity: Uncommon
Speaking Stones come in pairs, with each stone carved to match the other.
While you touch one stone, you can take a Magic action to send a short message of 25 words or fewer to the bearer of the other stone. That creature hears the message in its mind, recognizes you as the sender if it knows you, and can answer in a like manner immediately. The stones enable creatures to understand the meaning of your message. You can send the message across any distance. If no creature bears the other stone, you know that fact as soon as you use the stone, and you don't send the message. Once either stone is used to send a message in this way, the stones can't be used again until the next dawn. If one of the stones in a pair is destroyed, the other one becomes nonmagical.
Source: Stranger Things: Welcome to the Hellfire Club 2024

note.name: Adventure Info
note.text:
Ballad of the Rat King is an adventure for Level 3 Characters.
This adventure is designed to fill one or two sessions of play.
If you aren't using the Map of Greyhawkins, it can take place in any port town.

note.name: Key Plot Points
note.text:
This information is key to the adventure's story:
This nautical-themed tavern caters to dockworkers, deck hands, and other dockside regulars. Its two human proprietors are siblings and former sailors, Jay Le'Buck and Jeeves Whalington (Medium, Chaotic Good Commoners). "Captain" Jeeves spends his days daydreaming about adventures at sea and entertaining patrons. His sister, Jay, handles the real work that keeps the business afloat. She tallies the day's sales on a small chalkboard.
Plot Point 1. A guard named Fillius hires the characters to investigate a string of murders along the docks. The culprit is Korex, an ex-adventurer who commands a growing horde of sewer-dwelling rats with his magic pan flute.
Plot Point 2. Deputized by Fillius as temporary members of the Oakshore Guard, the characters question townsfolk and search the dockside neighborhood for clues.
Plot Point 3. After reporting their findings to Fillius, the characters set a trap for Korex. They confront Korex when he emerges from the sewers on the night of their investigation.

note.name: DM Preparation
note.text:
Before running the adventure, prepare as follows:
Step 1. Read the "Adventure Background" section.
Step 2. Familiarize yourself with the Oakshore Docks Map and the Writ of Authority Handout.
Step 4. Familiarize yourself with the monsters and non-player characters in this adventure, which are as follows:
6 Commoners 1 Cultist of Demogorgon 3 Demobats 3 Giant Corrupted Rats 1 Guard Korex 3 Swarms of Corrupted Rats
Step 5. Familiarize yourself with the magic items in this adventure, which are as follows:
Cloak of Billowing Holly's Handy Haversack Pipes of Pestilence Potion of Healing Potion of Resistance (Acid) Speaking Stones

note.name: Eddy Note
note.text: This adventure is a cautionary tale for players who like to steal from their party members. Let this adventure serve as a warning—you can play whatever kind of character you want, but you must play nicely with others at the table.

note.name: Player Assistance
note.text:
You can simplify the DM's role by having the other players handle tasks typically assigned to the DM. Before play begins, work with the other players to divide the following tasks among them:
Monster Wranglers. Whenever the characters encounter Hostile monsters, one or two players can run those monsters using their linked Stat Blocks. (If you have two players doing this, they can take turns.)
Rules Consultant. One player references the D&D Beyond Basic Rules as needed.

note.name: Adventure Background
note.text:
Korex the Cunning was once an adventurer and a musician who inspired his companions through song. But Korex greed outweighed his heroism. Every quest became a potential heist, an opportunity for Korex to steal from his fellow adventurers or the folk who sought their aid. Eventually, Korex's treachery was revealed. His betrayed companions ousted him, reclaimed what stolen wealth remained, and set off without him.
Korex remained in Oakshore with nothing but his pan flute. He quickly earned himself the reputation of a washed-up performer who never repaid his debts. Humiliated and penniless, Korex retreated to Oakshore's sewers, where he found company in his only remaining audience: rats.
In his isolation, Korex has learned how to control the rats, infecting them with defiling magic channeled through his pan flute. Enthralled by Korex's pestilent song, the rats swarm the pier at night, preying on all who spurned Korex.
With the captain of the local guard away on official business, her diffident second-in-command turns to the characters for aid, hoping to deputize them as detectives to lead an official investigation.

note.name: Beginning the Adventure
note.text:
When the players are ready to begin the adventure, read or paraphrase the following text:
"An urgent call for aid has brought you to the quiet port town of Oakshore. When you arrive, the harbor is mostly empty, no doubt due to recent reports of dock workers being slain at night. A stocky dwarf in a guard uniform sits on the curb, groaning and rubbing his temples. He leaps to his feet when he spots you.
"It's about time!" he exclaims. "Captain Skipper is away on business. I didn't know who else to call. Over the past week, three dock workers have been slain in the night. I need you to bring the killer to justice." "
The dwarf is the inept second-in-command of Oakshire's local guard, Fillius Kellaghan (Medium, Neutral Good Guard). Fillius has a nonchalant demeanor and a noticeable lack of gusto. While the captain is away, Fillius can hire adventurers as temporary members of the town guard, whose ranks are already spread thin.
Fillius imparts the following information:
Curfew. Fillius has issued a strict curfew. Locals must remain indoors until the case is solved.
Dead Workers. The victims were discovered at the end of their shifts. All their bodies were riddled with tiny holes. Fillius had the bodies burned as a precautionary measure.
Trade Stoppage. Fillius closed the docks, halting trade. Oakshore can't afford the closure for long.
Fillius offers the party 200 GP to gather evidence related to these crimes, plus an additional 100 GP if the characters apprehend or eliminate whoever or whatever is responsible for these grisly acts.

note.name: Honorary Investigators
note.text: If the characters accept the assignment, Fillius deputizes them as temporary members of the Oakshore Guard in a brief and underwhelming curbside ceremony.

note.name: Authority of Oakshore
note.text:
To aid the characters in their investigation, Fillius provides them with a document that authorizes them to question locals, enter any location along the dockside neighborhood, and, if necessary, use force to protect the townsfolk. Show the Oakshore Writ of Authority to the players when they are deputized.
Fillius also provides the characters one of his two Speaking Stones and tells them to contact him with the stone when they're ready to present their findings.

note.name: Conducting the Investigation
note.text:
The characters are free to conduct the investigation however they see fit. Fillius can offer the following suggestions for ways to gather clues:
Entering Locations. The characters have permission to enter any of the dock's locations, although they might have to present the writ of authority to convince an occupant to permit them entry.
Looking for Clues. The characters can search the docks for physical evidence related to the dock workers' deaths.
Questioning Witnesses. The characters can talk to the locals. Fillius cautions the characters not to act brashly and to keep the peace whenever possible. He trusts them to conduct a thorough investigation rooted in sound evidence, integrity, and even tempers.
At your discretion, Fillius might remain nearby to offer additional information or a second opinion to the party during the adventure. Otherwise, he wishes the characters good luck and departs to fill out reports.

note.name: Eddy Note
note.text:
Running a mystery can be hard work. No matter where the characters go, they should always find at least one clue. When the characters talk to the locals, you can suggest questions for them to ask, like these:
"What did you see on the nights of the murders?"
"Aside from the murders, have you noticed anything unusual or suspicious lately?"
"Who or what do you think is to blame?"

note.name: Oakshore Docks
note.text: Normally a bustling hub of business and trade, the Oakshore docks are devoid of activity.

note.name: Starting the Investigation
note.text:
Refer to the Oakshore Docks Map and identify the Suggested Start Area for the Player Characters. Don't reveal the rest of the dock's contents yet, as they aren't initially visible to the characters.
To begin the investigation, read or paraphrase:
"Oakshore's dockside neighborhood consists of six single-story homes and businesses separated by dingy alleys. Shells in intricate geometrical patterns adorn the facade of each building. Across the cobblestone street, flagstone steps descend to a rickety pier and a gloomy harbor. The neighborhood is eerily still. The only sounds come from the pier, where tides splash against barnacle-laden beams. Where do you want to go first?"
Have the players choose a location shown on the map. The characters can visit locations in any order, and they don't have to visit all of them to complete their investigation.
When the characters have enough information to present their findings to Fillius, proceed to "Ending the Investigation." If you'd like to shorten this adventure, you can have Fillius contact the characters using his Speaking Stone after they've explored three or four locations, which should provide them with enough information to make an educated guess about the killer's identity.

note.name: Oakshore Docks Features
note.text:
Unless otherwise specified, the docks have the following features:
Ceilings. Ceilings are 10 feet high inside businesses and residences.
Doors. Doors throughout the docks are made from palm wood planks banded with iron. Unless otherwise noted, doors are unlocked. As a Utilize action, a character can try to pick a locked door using Thieves' Tools; a successful DC 15 Dexterity (Sleight of Hand) check opens it. Alternatively, a character can take an action to try to force open a locked door, doing so with a successful DC 15 Strength (Athletics) check.
Rattraps. Oakshore's residents have scattered rattraps throughout the docks in response to a sudden rodent infestation.

note.name: Ending the Investigation
note.text:
The investigation ends when the characters feel they've collected enough information to report to Fillius, who meets them after they contact him with the Speaking Stone he gave them.
If the characters correctly deduce that Korex is behind the murders, Fillius agrees that the evidence supports their conclusion. If the characters don't arrive at this discovery themselves, Fillius can help them make the connection. Regardless, Fillius also recounts that Korex is a former adventurer who was ousted for his thievery (as detailed in this adventure's background).

note.name: Rattrap
note.text:
Korex will try to strike again. To catch the piper in the act, Fillius recommends the characters set a trap. The characters might confront Korex at any of the following locations:
Alley. Korex emerges from the sewers via the grate in the alley (area 06). The characters could hide amid the trash cans and ambush him there.
Abandoned House. If the characters explored Korex's house (areas 07a and 07b), they might decide to wait for him there.
Pier. All the murders happened at the pier (area 08). The characters could use the crane and its net to catch Korex in the act.
Once the characters agree on a plan and make preparations, allow them to take a Short Rest, then proceed to the following section.

note.name: Conclusion
note.text: After the characters defeat Korex and his swarms, they can return to Fillius, who commends them on their valiant efforts and keen detective work. Fillius collects the writ of authority and pays the characters 200 GP for the evidence against Korex, as well as an additional 100 GP if they apprehended or dispatched the murderer. As a further token of gratitude, Fillius lets the characters keep both Speaking Stones to aid them in future endeavors.

trait.name: Source
trait.text: Stranger Things: Welcome to the Hellfire Club 2024

action.name: Multiattack
action.text: Dardew makes two attacks, using Fiery Claw or Power Chord in any combination.

action.name: Fiery Claw
action.text: Melee Attack Roll: +7, reach 5 ft. Hit: 8 (1d8 + 4) Slashing damage plus 7 (2d6) Fire damage.

action.name: Power Chord
action.text: Constitution Saving Throw: DC 14, one creature Dardew can see within 120 ft. Failure: 13 (3d6 + 3) Thunder damage.

action.name: Silver Tongue (bonus action)

note.name: Read
note.text:
As the smoke clears, you find yourself in a pit within a hellish arena. The temperature is oppressively hot, and the air stinks of sulfur. Thousands of devils leer down at you from rows of seating above a twisted cage of infernal steel. Diabolical music fills the arena.
You stand on a raised platform inscribed with a pentagram. Red flames burn in five braziers, one at each of the star's points.
Dardew flies over the pit. His voice booms out, quieting the din. "Welcome, one and all, to the Hellfire Dome! Prepare yourselves for a night of death, betrayal, and vile spectacle." The audience erupts in thunderous applause.
"I present to you our latest contenders: group five hundred twenty-seven! Will they survive to become the Hellfire Club's newest initiates? Only time will tell, but we wish them luck. Let the Hellfire Games begin!"
Suddenly, five eyeless, flying demons burst from the fiery braziers.

note.name: Info
note.text:
The five Demobats fight to the death. Whenever the characters vanquish a Demobat, the audience rewards them (see "Treasure" below). Once the characters defeat the bats, five blazing portals appear along the arena's walls.
Dardew then floats down and congratulates the characters on completing their first challenge. He informs them that only after they have completed at least two more challenges can they proceed through the east portal to their final test (area H7). Dardew doesn't reveal what lies beyond any of the portals.

note.name: Portals
note.text:
Five portals in the arena lead to the following locations:
Portal A. Portal A leads to area H4. Portal B. Portal B leads to area H3. Portal C. Portal C leads to area H7. The portal doesn't function until the characters have completed challenges in at least two other areas. Portal D. Portal D leads to area H5. Portal E. Portal E leads to area H2.
Each portal initially functions as a one-way door, preventing reentry until the characters have completed the challenge in the destination area.

note.name: Treasure
note.text: Each time a character kills a Demobat, the audience throws 50 GP into this area.

item.name: Gold Piece (GP) [5.5e]
item.text:
Characters often find coins on their adventures and can spend those coins in shops, inns, and other businesses. Coins come in different denominations based on the relative worth of their material. The Coin Values table lists coins and how much they're worth relative to the Gold Piece, which is the game's main coin. For example, 100 Copper Pieces are worth 1 Gold Piece. A coin weighs about a third of an ounce, so fifty coins weigh a pound.
Coin | Value in GP Copper Piece (CP) | 1/100 Silver Piece (SP) | 1/10 Electrum Piece (EP) | 1/2 Gold Piece (GP) | 1 Platinum Piece (PP) | 10
Source: Player's Handbook 2024 p. 213

note.name: Read
note.text:
Dardew hovers impatiently over this room, which features two rusty, wrought-iron podiums that resemble corroded coffins. Five masked cultists stand behind one of the podiums, while the other is vacant.
"It's that time again!" Dardew announces to the devils above. "Are you ready for another game of Fiend Feud?" The audience cheers. "Contestants, take your places." Dardew clears his throat and gestures to the empty podium.

note.name: Info
note.text:
This challenge is called Fiend Feud, a trivia game of brains and bloodshed where two teams compete for fame, fortune—and their lives. The opposing team consists of five Cultists of Demogorgon who are Hostile toward the characters. Dardew acts as host.
Once the characters take their places behind the podium, the game begins.

note.name: Playing Fiend Feud
note.text:
Fiend Feud is a trivia game with three rounds and a mostly arbitrary points system. The game's rules are as follows:
Trivia Questions. Each round, Dardew asks the characters one question from the Fiend Feud Questions table, rerolling duplicates. These questions test player knowledge as much as character knowledge. The player characters are allowed to briefly deliberate before giving their response. If they answer the question correctly, their team receives 1 point.
Chance to Steal. If the characters answer a question incorrectly, the opposing team has a chance answer the question, receiving 1 point if they answer it correctly. The cultists are well-versed in occult lore and answer any such questions correctly, but they know little about anything else and get those questions wrong. The cultists know the answers to questions 1, 4, and 6 in the Fiend Feud Questions table.
Bonus Round. After three rounds of trivia, both teams can earn bonus points by killing members of the opposing team. Have everyone roll Initiative. For each opposing team member they slay, a team earns 1 point.
The game ends when only one team remains.

note.name: Eddy Note
note.text: If you really want to run a certain encounter, you can just have whichever portal the characters go through lead to that area. It's an old DM trick I like to call "the invisible railroad."

note.name: Fiend Feud Questions
note.text:
1d6 | Question Answer 1 | What vile lich is missing one eye and one hand? Vecna 2 | What creatures love brains but loathe zombies? Mind flayers 3 | Which vampire lord rules over the land of Barovia? Count Strahd von Zarovich 4 | Which demon lord is the self-styled Prince of Demons? Demogorgon 5 | Which warrior severed the hand of a famous lich? Kas 6 | Which archdevil rules over all the Nine Hells? Asmodeus
At the DM's discretion, a character can take the Study action to make a DC 15 Intelligence (Arcana or Religion) check to know the answer to a question, learning the correct answer on a successful check.

note.name: Treasure
note.text:
Characters receive treasure as follows:
Correct Answers. Whenever a character correctly answers a question, the audience throws 10 GP into this area.
Slaying Cultists. Whenever a character slays a cultist, the audience throws 25 GP into this area.
Winning the Game. If the characters win Fiend Feud, the audience showers them with 150 GP.

trait.name: Treasure
trait.text: Armaments. The monster's treasure hoard features magic items with the noted theme. The Dungeon Master's Guide details treasure hoards appropriate for each theme.

trait.name: Magic Resistance
trait.text: The devil has Advantage on saving throws against spells and other magical effects.

action.name: Multiattack
action.text: The devil makes one Beard attack and one Infernal Glaive attack.

action.name: Beard
action.text: Melee Attack Roll: +5, reach 5 ft. Hit: 7 (1d8 + 3) Piercing damage, and the target has the Poisoned condition until the start of the devil's next turn. Until this poison ends, the target can't regain Hit Points.

action.name: Infernal Glaive
action.text: Melee Attack Roll: +5, reach 10 ft. Hit: 8 (1d10 + 3) Slashing damage. If the target is a creature and doesn't already have an infernal wound, it is subjected to the following effect. Constitution Saving Throw: DC 12. Failure: The target receives an infernal wound. While wounded, the target loses 5 (1d10) Hit Points at the start of each of its turns. The wound closes after 1 minute, after a spell restores Hit Points to the target, or after the target or a creature within 5 feet of it takes an action to stanch the wound, doing so by succeeding on a DC 12 Wisdom (Medicine) check.

trait.name: Source
trait.text: Stranger Things: Welcome to the Hellfire Club 2024

trait.name: Magic Resistance
trait.text: The imp has Advantage on saving throws against spells and other magical effects.

action.name: Sting
action.text: Melee Attack Roll: +5, reach 5 ft. Hit: 6 (1d6 + 3) Piercing damage plus 7 (2d6) Poison damage.

action.name: Invisibility
action.text: The imp has the Invisible condition for 1 minute. This effect ends early immediately after the imp makes an attack roll or deals damage, or if the imp has the Incapacitated condition.

action.name: Shape-Shift
action.text: The imp shape-shifts to resemble a rat (Speed 20 ft.), a raven (20 ft., fly 60 ft.), or a spider (20 ft., Climb 20 ft.), or it returns to its true form. Its statistics are the same in each form, except for its Speed. Any equipment it is wearing or carrying isn't transformed.

note.name: Read
note.text:
This fetid chamber holds a bubbling well of brownish sludge. In front of the well, a devil stands poised for battle. Glaive in hand, the devil sports a writhing beard of barbed, snakelike appendages.
"Fight well alongside me, maggots," it spits, "Or I'll stick you like the smelly flesh bags you are!" Just then, the well burps up six squealing demons, each more hideous than the last.
An unseen creature whispers in your ear. "Kill the demons, then kill this devil—make it look like an accident, and you'll be rewarded. You have my word!"

note.name: DM Secret!
note.text: Don't reveal the Imp Trickster yet. This monster is invisible.

note.name: Info
note.text:
Six Dretches erupt from the well. The Hostile demons fight the characters and the Bearded Devil, which is initially Indifferent toward the party and fights alongside them. Once all six dretches have been slain, the challenge is complete, and the portal to area H1 reopens.
A treacherous Imp Trickster with the Invisible condition watches the battle unfold. The imp loathes the bearded devil and wishes it dead but is Indifferent toward the characters. During the battle, the imp encourages characters to turn on the bearded devil when the last dretch falls.

note.name: The Devil You Don't Know
note.text: The bearded devil is an overbearing grunt who barks orders at the characters and scolds any who defy its commands. Whenever a character slays a demon, the bearded devil loudly takes credit for the kill.

note.name: Treasure
note.text:
Characters receive treasure as follows:
Defeating the Bearded Devil. If a character kills the bearded devil, the imp turns visible and gives them 200 GP.
Defeating Dretches. Each time a character kills a dretch, the audience throws 50 GP into this area.
Defeating the Imp. If a character kills the Imp Trickster, the bearded devil thanks the characters for eliminating "the spy" and gives them 10 GP.

monster.label: Flail (Trap)
monster.name: Flail (Trap)
monster.conditionImmune: Blinded, Charmed, Deafened, Exhaustion, Frightened, Grappled, Incapacitated, Paralyzed, Petrified, Poisoned, Prone, Restrained, Stunned, Surprised, Unconscious

action.name: Crash
action.text: Each creature within 10 feet of the flail must succeed on a DC 15 Dexterity saving throw or take 10 (3d6) Bludgeoning damage and have the Prone condition. The flail doesn't harm the gnolls.

note.name: Read
note.text:
Three mangy, hyenoid fiends conduct a ritual in this wicked sanctuary. In the center of the chamber, a giant, wicked-looking flail spins and stops at unpredictable intervals, violently swinging its three spiked heads.
Two of the creatures cackle maliciously as the third—completely hairless—chants, "Yeenoghu! Deliver us from this wretched game so we may spill blood in your name!"

note.name: Info
note.text:
Three Gnoll Warriors beseech the demon lord Yeenoghu for aid in this sanctuary. They are Hostile toward all other creatures and rush them on sight.
These are the same gnolls from the Dungeon of Shadows (see "The Vanishing Gnome"). They have since died and made their way to the Hellfire Dome. If the characters fought them previously, the gnolls recognize them and greet them accordingly:
"The gnolls visibly seethe with hatred. "You again?!" shouts the hairless gnoll. "Heretics! How dare you come here and interrupt our ritual a second time!" "
The gnolls fight to the death (again).

note.name: Flail
note.text:
The spinning flail is embedded in the ground between the gnolls. Each round on Initiative count 10 (losing Initiative ties), the flail's three heads crash to the ground. Each creature within 10 feet of the flail must succeed on a DC 15 Dexterity saving throw or take 3d6 Bludgeoning damage and have the Prone condition. The flail doesn't harm the gnolls.
The flail has AC 18, HP 40, and Immunity to Poison and Psychic damage. If the flail is destroyed, it stops spinning, and the audience roars in jubilation.

note.name: Treasure
note.text:
Characters receive treasure as follows:
Defeating Gnolls. Each time a character kills a gnoll, the audience throws 100 GP into this area.
Destroying the Flail. If a character destroys the flail, the audience throws 200 GP into this area.

A coin weighs about a third of an ounce, so fifty coins weigh a pound.
